explanation.whyCorrect: Some SCLC tumors overexpress BCL2, an anti-apoptotic protein. BCL2 suppresses programmed cell death (apoptosis), allowing cancerous cells to survive despite DNA damage. Combined with loss of tumor suppressors (TP53, RB) and MYC amplification, BCL2 overexpression contributes to the aggressive behavior of SCLC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: BCL2 does not promote DNA repair. It prevents apoptosis, allowing cells with damaged DNA to survive.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BCL2 does not directly increase angiogenesis. Other factors (VEGF) drive new blood vessel formation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: BCL2's mechanism is anti-apoptotic, not immune evasion. Immune checkpoint molecules (PD-L1) are involved in immune evasion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: BCL2 does not activate cell cycle checkpoints. TP53 and RB are the checkpoint regulators. BCL2 prevents the death of cells that bypass these checkpoints.

questions.stem: Immunotherapy agents atezolizumab and pembrolizumab may be added to chemotherapy for SCLC. These agents target which of the following immune checkpoint pathways?

explanation.summary: Atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1) and pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) block the PD-1/PD-L1 immune checkpoint pathway, allowing T cells to attack tumor cells that express PD-L1 to evade immune detection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atezolizumab and pembrolizumab are immune checkpoint inhibitors that target the PD-1/PD-L1 pathway. Atezolizumab blocks PD-L1 on tumor cells, while pembrolizumab blocks PD-1 on T cells. By blocking this checkpoint, T cells are reactivated to attack tumor cells. These agents may be added to cisplatin/etoposide chemotherapy for SCLC based on genetic testing results.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CTLA-4 pathway is targeted by ipilimumab, not atezolizumab or pembrolizumab.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CD28/B7 costimulation is a T cell activation pathway, not the target of these checkpoint inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IL-2 receptor pathway is targeted by different immunotherapies (basiliximab, aldesleukin), not PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: TNF-Î± signaling is targeted by anti-TNF biologics (infliximab, adalimumab) for autoimmune diseases, not cancer immunotherapy.

questions.stem: All SCLC tumors are considered which of the following grades?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Benign with potential for malignant transformation

explanation.summary: All SCLC tumors are high grade with a high mitotic rate and rapid growth. They often outgrow their blood supply, leading to central necrosis (Azzopardi effect). This aggressive behavior explains the poor prognosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: All small cell lung tumors are high grade, with a high mitotic rate and rapid growth. They frequently outgrow their blood supply, causing central necrosis. This aggressive biology explains why SCLC is the most aggressive lung cancer type, often metastatic at diagnosis, with median survival of only 8-20 months.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SCLC is never low grade. Bronchial carcinoids are the low-grade neuroendocrine tumors. SCLC is always high grade.
whyWrongByChoice.B: SCLC is not intermediate grade. It is uniformly high grade.
whyWrongByChoice.D: All SCLC is high grade regardless of stage. Grade and stage are different concepts.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SCLC is always malignant. Benign neuroendocrine tumors of the lung are carcinoids, not SCLC.

questions.stem: SCLC is thought to originate from which of the following cell types in the bronchial wall?

explanation.summary: SCLC originates from bronchial neuroendocrine cells, which are normal constituents of the airway that secrete various amines and polypeptide hormones. This origin explains the neuroendocrine markers and paraneoplastic hormone secretion.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC is thought to originate from bronchial neuroendocrine cells, which are normal constituents of the airway that secrete various amines and polypeptide hormones like DOPA. This neuroendocrine origin explains why SCLC cells express neuroendocrine markers (chromogranin, synaptophysin) and can secrete ectopic hormones (ADH, ACTH) causing paraneoplastic syndromes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ciliated columnar epithelial cells are the predominant cell type in the respiratory epithelium but do not give rise to SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Goblet cells produce mucus and do not give rise to SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant and are the cell of origin for some adenocarcinomas, not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Basal stem cells can give rise to squamous cell carcinoma (through squamous metaplasia), not specifically SCLC.

questions.stem: When the cancer is limited (confined to one lung and nearby lymph nodes), SCLC is treated with chemotherapy combined with radiation, with the goal of which of the following?

explanation.summary: For limited SCLC, chemotherapy and radiation together are used as initial therapy with the goal of curing the cancer. For extensive disease, chemotherapy alone is used to slow growth, reduce symptoms, and improve survival (not curative).
explanation.whyCorrect: For limited SCLC (cancer in only one lung or nearby lymph nodes), chemotherapy and radiation therapy can be used together as initial therapy with the goal of curing the cancer. However, for extensive (widespread) disease, chemotherapy is used alone to slow cancer growth, reduce symptoms, and improve survival, without the expectation of cure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In limited disease, the treatment goal is curative, not purely palliative. Palliation is the goal for extensive disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The combined chemo-radiation approach is used to cure the cancer, not to prevent surgical complications.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While paraneoplastic symptoms do improve with treatment, the primary goal for limited disease is cancer cure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Most SCLC patients do not undergo surgery. The chemo-radiation regimen is the definitive treatment for limited disease, not preparation for surgery.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old woman with a nagging cough and hemoptysis has an elevated serum calcium. Chest X-ray reveals a central pulmonary nodule. Biopsy shows sheets of malignant squamous epithelium with keratin pearls and intercellular bridging. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Squamous cell carcinoma shows sheets of malignant squamous epithelium with keratin pearls and intercellular bridging. It is centrally located and associated with hypercalcemia (PTHrP). Strong smoking association.
explanation.whyCorrect: The central location, keratin pearls, intercellular bridging, and hypercalcemia are classic for squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the lung. SCC originates from flat squamous epithelial cells lining the airways, typically grows centrally, and is associated with PTHrP-mediated hypercalcemia as a paraneoplastic syndrome. SCC has the strongest smoking association among NSCLC subtypes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenocarcinoma shows glandular differentiation (mucin-producing glands/ducts), not keratin pearls. It is typically peripheral, not central.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Large cell carcinoma shows large cells with prominent nucleoli but lacks squamous or glandular differentiation by definition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Small cell carcinoma shows small cells with scant cytoplasm and salt-and-pepper chromatin, not keratin pearls.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carcinoid tumors are low-grade neuroendocrine tumors with neuroendocrine markers, not keratin pearls.

questions.stem: Adenocarcinoma is the most common subtype of NSCLC and is also the most common lung cancer in which of the following patient populations?

explanation.summary: While smoking is a risk factor for all lung cancers, adenocarcinoma is the most common lung cancer subtype detected in nonsmokers. It arises from mucus-producing epithelial cells and can occur without smoking exposure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pulmonary adenocarcinoma is the most common lung cancer subtype detected in nonsmokers. While smoking is still a strong risk factor, adenocarcinoma can develop without smoking exposure. This is in contrast to squamous cell carcinoma and SCLC, which have even stronger smoking associations.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Heavy smokers are at risk for all lung cancers but squamous cell carcinoma and SCLC have the strongest smoking associations. The specific distinction is that adenocarcinoma is most common in nonsmokers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Asbestos exposure increases risk for lung cancer (especially with concurrent smoking) and mesothelioma, but nonsmoker status is the specific population where adenocarcinoma predominates.
whyWrongByChoice.D: COPD is a risk factor for lung cancer broadly, not specifically adenocarcinoma in nonsmokers.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Age is a risk factor for all lung cancers, not specifically adenocarcinoma in nonsmokers.

questions.stem: Adenocarcinoma and large cell carcinoma are typically found in which location within the lung?

explanation.summary: Adenocarcinoma and large cell carcinoma are typically peripheral lung tumors. In contrast, squamous cell carcinoma and SCLC are centrally located (hilar). Location provides a clue to histologic subtype.
explanation.whyCorrect: Adenocarcinomas and large cell carcinomas are most typically found in the lung periphery. In contrast, squamous cell carcinomas are usually found more centrally in the bronchial airways (similar to SCLC). This location difference provides an important clue to the underlying histology when evaluating imaging.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Central (hilar) location is characteristic of squamous cell carcinoma and SCLC, not adenocarcinoma or large cell carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The pleural surface is the location of mesothelioma, not typically adenocarcinoma or large cell carcinoma primary tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mediastinal lymph nodes may be involved by metastasis but are not the typical primary tumor location.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The tracheal bifurcation (carina) is not the typical primary site of adenocarcinoma or large cell carcinoma.

questions.stem: A patient with lung adenocarcinoma has molecular testing revealing an EGFR mutation. Which of the following targeted therapies would be most appropriate?

explanation.summary: For EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma, EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors like osimertinib terminate cancer cell growth by specifically targeting the mutated receptor. These work better than conventional chemotherapy for EGFR-positive tumors.
explanation.whyCorrect: For lung adenocarcinoma with EGFR mutations, specific tyrosine kinase inhibitors targeting EGFR (osimertinib) are indicated. These drugs terminate cancer cell growth by specifically targeting the mutated EGFR receptor. They work better than conventional chemotherapy for EGFR-positive tumors. Adverse effects include skin issues, mouth sores, fatigue, and muscle/joint pain.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alectinib targets ALK, not EGFR. It would be appropriate for ALK-rearranged adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pembrolizumab is a PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor. While it may be used in NSCLC, EGFR-mutant tumors are specifically treated with EGFR TKIs first.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cisplatin is a general chemotherapy agent. While it may be part of the regimen, the targeted EGFR TKI is the specific therapy for EGFR-mutant tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Etoposide is used in SCLC (with cisplatin), not as targeted therapy for EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: The progression from normal bronchial epithelium to invasive squamous cell carcinoma follows a stepwise sequence. Which of the following correctly orders these stages?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dysplasia â†’ metaplasia â†’ carcinoma in situ â†’ invasion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Metaplasia â†’ dysplasia â†’ carcinoma in situ â†’ invasion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hyperplasia â†’ metaplasia â†’ invasion â†’ carcinoma in situ

choices.label: D
choices.text: Carcinoma in situ â†’ metaplasia â†’ dysplasia â†’ invasion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Invasion â†’ metaplasia â†’ dysplasia â†’ carcinoma in situ

explanation.summary: The progression to invasive SCC follows: normal epithelium â†’ squamous metaplasia (from smoking) â†’ squamous dysplasia â†’ carcinoma in situ (full-thickness dysplasia) â†’ invasive SCC (basement membrane penetration).
explanation.whyCorrect: Squamous cell carcinoma develops through a stepwise progression: normal bronchial columnar epithelium â†’ squamous metaplasia (from cigarette smoke exposure) â†’ squamous dysplasia (with continued exposure) â†’ carcinoma in situ (full-thickness dysplasia without basement membrane invasion) â†’ invasive squamous cell carcinoma (basement membrane penetration with ability to invade and metastasize).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dysplasia does not precede metaplasia. Metaplasia (change in cell type) occurs first, followed by dysplasia (abnormal cell growth) within the metaplastic epithelium.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Invasion does not precede carcinoma in situ. CIS is defined as full-thickness dysplasia WITHOUT invasion; invasion occurs as the next step.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CIS does not precede metaplasia. The sequence starts with metaplasia and progresses toward CIS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Invasion is the final step, not the first.

questions.stem: Lung adenocarcinoma can be distinguished from metastatic adenocarcinoma to the lung by immunohistochemical staining. Which of the following markers are positive in primary lung adenocarcinoma?

explanation.summary: Primary lung adenocarcinoma stains positive for TTF-1 (thyroid transcription factor-1), CK7 (cytokeratin 7), and Napsin A. These markers distinguish it from adenocarcinoma metastatic to the lung from other organs.
explanation.whyCorrect: Primary lung adenocarcinoma is distinguished from metastatic adenocarcinoma by immunohistochemistry. Lung adenocarcinoma stains positive for TTF-1 (thyroid transcription factor-1), CK7 (cytokeratin 7), and Napsin A. Metastatic adenocarcinoma from other organs (stomach, colon) would be negative for these lung-specific markers.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CK5/6 and p63 are markers of squamous cell differentiation (SCC stains positive for CK7, CK5/6, p40, p63), not adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chromogranin and synaptophysin are neuroendocrine markers for SCLC and carcinoid tumors, not adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CD20 and CD79a are B cell markers for lymphoma, not lung cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: S-100 and HMB-45 are melanoma markers.

questions.stem: KRAS mutations are found in approximately 25% of lung adenocarcinomas. These mutations affect a GTPase-coding gene and are associated with which of the following prognoses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excellent prognosis with high cure rates

explanation.summary: KRAS mutations (found in ~25% of adenocarcinomas) affect a GTPase-coding gene and are associated with poor survival. Mutations in KRAS contribute to malignant transformation and reduce overall survival by about 25%.
explanation.whyCorrect: KRAS mutations are found in approximately 25% of lung adenocarcinoma cases and affect a GTPase-coding gene involved in cell signaling. KRAS mutations are associated with poor survivalâ€”they contribute to malignant transformation of cells and reduce overall survival by about 25%.

whyWrongByChoice.A: KRAS mutations are associated with poor survival, not excellent prognosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: KRAS mutations do affect prognosisâ€”they are associated with worse outcomes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While newer KRAS-targeted therapies are being developed, KRAS mutations have historically been associated with poor prognosis, not improved immunotherapy response.
whyWrongByChoice.E: KRAS-mutant tumors do not spontaneously regress. They tend to be aggressive.

questions.stem: Large cell carcinoma can be considered a diagnosis of exclusion. Which of the following histologic features define it?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Large cells with prominent nucleoli lacking squamous or glandular differentiation

explanation.summary: Large cell carcinoma is an undifferentiated malignant epithelial tumor with large cells, large nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and moderate cytoplasmâ€”but lacking evidence of glandular or squamous differentiation or SCLC features.
explanation.whyCorrect: Large cell carcinoma is defined by what it is NOT: it lacks glandular differentiation (adenocarcinoma), squamous differentiation (SCC), and features of small cell carcinoma. It shows large cells with large nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and moderate amounts of cytoplasm. It is a diagnosis of exclusion when other NSCLC subtypes are ruled out histologically.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glandular formation with mucin production defines adenocarcinoma, not large cell carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Keratin pearls with intercellular bridging define squamous cell carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Small cells with salt-and-pepper chromatin define SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Well-differentiated neuroendocrine morphology describes carcinoid tumors, not large cell carcinoma (though neuroendocrine variants of LCC exist).

questions.stem: The overall 5-year survival rate for NSCLC is approximately which of the following?

explanation.summary: The overall 5-year survival for NSCLC is about 25%. However, if detected early and localized to the lung, survival increases to ~50%. Distant metastasis drops survival to ~6%.
explanation.whyCorrect: Despite newer therapies, NSCLC generally has a poor prognosis. The overall 5-year survival rate is about 25%, but increases to approximately 50% if the cancer is detected early and is localized to the lung. Distant metastasis is associated with a 5-year survival of only about 6%. These numbers are improving with modern targeted therapies.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 5% is the 5-year survival for distant metastatic disease (~6%), not overall NSCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 50% is the survival rate for early, localized NSCLC, not overall.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 75% overestimates the overall 5-year survival for NSCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 90% greatly overestimates NSCLC survival.

questions.stem: Alectinib is a targeted therapy used for which specific molecular alteration in lung adenocarcinoma?

explanation.summary: Alectinib is an ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitor used for lung adenocarcinoma with ALK rearrangement. ALK-positive patients are often younger, light or nonsmokers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alectinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor specifically targeting ALK (anaplastic lymphoma kinase). It is used for lung adenocarcinoma patients with ALK rearrangement, who tend to be younger, light or nonsmokers. Common adverse effects include fatigue, swelling, constipation, muscle pain, weight gain, vision changes, and photosensitivity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: EGFR mutations are treated with osimertinib (EGFR TKI), not alectinib.
whyWrongByChoice.B: KRAS mutations have been historically difficult to target, though newer KRAS inhibitors are being developed. Alectinib targets ALK.
whyWrongByChoice.D: TP53 deletion does not have a specific targeted therapy. It affects tumor suppressor function broadly.
whyWrongByChoice.E: BRAF V600E mutations are treated with BRAF inhibitors (dabrafenib/trametinib), not alectinib.

questions.stem: SCC of the lung originates from which of the following cell types?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flat squamous epithelial cells of the airways

explanation.summary: Squamous cell carcinoma originates in the flat squamous epithelial cells lining the lungs' airways. It develops through squamous metaplasia â†’ dysplasia â†’ CIS â†’ invasive carcinoma from chronic cigarette smoke exposure.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCC originates in the flat squamous epithelial cells lining the lungs' airways. Normally, the bronchial epithelium is columnar, but chronic cigarette smoke exposure causes squamous metaplasia, which can progress through dysplasia to carcinoma.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mucus-producing epithelial cells give rise to adenocarcinoma, not SCC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neuroendocrine progenitor cells give rise to SCLC and carcinoid tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant and are involved in some adenocarcinomas, not SCC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Alveolar macrophages are immune cells that do not give rise to lung carcinomas.

questions.stem: Squamous cell carcinoma in situ differs from invasive SCC by which of the following criteria?

explanation.summary: SCC in situ has full-thickness epithelial dysplasia with the same cytologic features as invasive carcinoma but has NOT penetrated the basement membrane. Once it invades beyond the basement membrane, it becomes invasive SCC.
explanation.whyCorrect: Squamous cell carcinoma in situ is defined as full-thickness dysplasia of the epithelium with the same cytologic nuclear features as invasive carcinoma, BUT the cells have NOT gained the ability to invade beyond the basement membrane. Once the carcinoma penetrates the basement membrane, it is classified as invasive SCC with the ability to invade adjacent structures and metastasize.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Both CIS and invasive SCC can have keratin formation. The distinguishing factor is basement membrane integrity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CIS and invasive SCC have similar cytologic atypia. The key difference is invasion, not atypia degree.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tumor size criteria (3 cm) is used in adenocarcinoma staging (to classify frank adenocarcinoma), not as the CIS vs. invasive SCC distinction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lymph node metastasis occurs with invasive disease but is a consequence of invasion, not the defining criterion. Basement membrane penetration defines invasive disease.

questions.stem: Smoking increases the risk of NSCLC up to 20-fold. After quitting smoking, the NSCLC risk decreases but even after 30 years remains approximately which of the following compared to nonsmokers?

explanation.summary: After quitting smoking, NSCLC risk slowly decreases but never reaches a nonsmoker's level. By 30 years after quitting, the risk is still approximately double that of nonsmokers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Smoking increases NSCLC risk up to 20-fold. After quitting, the risk slowly decreases over time but never fully returns to a nonsmoker's baseline. Even 30 years after quitting, the NSCLC risk remains approximately double that of people who never smoked.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The risk never fully returns to nonsmoker levels. It remains elevated even decades after cessation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: By 30 years, the risk has decreased substantially from the 20-fold elevation during active smoking to approximately double.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ten times would be the approximate risk during or shortly after active smoking, not 30 years post-cessation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Twenty times is the peak risk during active smoking, not the residual risk 30 years after quitting.

questions.stem: The three stages of progressive cell transformation in lung adenocarcinoma are, in order, which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Squamous metaplasia â†’ dysplasia â†’ invasion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atypical adenomatous hyperplasia â†’ adenocarcinoma in situ â†’ invasive adenocarcinoma

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carcinoma in situ â†’ hyperplasia â†’ invasion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bronchial metaplasia â†’ neuroendocrine differentiation â†’ invasion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Goblet cell hyperplasia â†’ mucin accumulation â†’ invasion

explanation.summary: Lung adenocarcinoma develops through: atypical adenomatous hyperplasia (small dysplastic pneumocyte foci) â†’ adenocarcinoma in situ (larger dysplastic areas) â†’ frank invasive adenocarcinoma (>3 cm with ability to invade and metastasize).
explanation.whyCorrect: The progressive cell transformations in adenocarcinoma are: (1) Atypical adenomatous hyperplasiaâ€”small areas of dysplastic pneumocytes; (2) Adenocarcinoma in situ (formerly bronchoalveolar carcinoma)â€”larger areas of dysplasia with increasingly atypical cytology; (3) Invasive adenocarcinomaâ€”lesion >3 cm with full ability to invade adjacent tissues and metastasize.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Squamous metaplasia â†’ dysplasia â†’ invasion describes the progression of squamous cell carcinoma, not adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This sequence is incorrectâ€”hyperplasia precedes CIS, not the reverse.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Neuroendocrine differentiation is the pathway for SCLC, not adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Goblet cell hyperplasia and mucin accumulation are not the standard progression stages of lung adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: TP53 gene mutations are the most common genetic alteration in squamous cell carcinoma of the lung. On which chromosome is TP53 located?

explanation.summary: TP53 is located on chromosome 17 and is the most commonly mutated tumor suppressor gene in SCC of the lung. Other commonly affected genes include CDKN2 (chromosome 9) and RB1 (chromosome 13).
explanation.whyCorrect: TP53 gene mutations, located on chromosome 17, are the most common genetic alterations in squamous cell carcinoma of the lung. Other commonly affected tumor suppressor genes include CDKN2 on chromosome 9 and the retinoblastoma tumor suppressor gene RB1 on chromosome 13. These deletions/inactivations are induced by carcinogens in cigarette smoke.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chromosome 3 is a site of deletions in SCC but TP53 is specifically on chromosome 17.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Chromosome 9 is the location of CDKN2, not TP53.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chromosome 13 is the location of RB1, not TP53.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chromosome 22 is not the location of TP53.

questions.stem: For small NSCLC tumors that are localized, the primary treatment with curative intent is which of the following?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Surgical resection followed by adjuvant therapy

explanation.summary: Small, localized NSCLC tumors (with only local lymph node extension) are treated with surgical resection followed by chemotherapy and/or targeted therapy. This offers the best chance of cure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Small tumors and those with only local extension to lymph nodes are treated with surgical resection, followed by chemotherapy and/or targeted therapy. This differs from SCLC, where surgery is rarely beneficial due to widespread metastasis. For NSCLC, surgery offers the best chance of cure when the cancer is detected early.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chemotherapy alone is not curative for localized NSCLC. Surgery is the primary curative modality.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Radiation alone may be used for patients who cannot undergo surgery but is not the primary treatment for resectable tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Immunotherapy alone is not the standard primary treatment for localized NSCLC. It may be part of adjuvant therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Watchful waiting is inappropriate for confirmed NSCLC. Treatment should be initiated promptly.

questions.stem: Pembrolizumab has significantly improved outcomes for many NSCLC patients. Which of the following is its mechanism of action?

explanation.summary: Pembrolizumab is a PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor that blocks the PD-1 receptor on T cells, preventing tumor-mediated immune evasion and allowing T cell-mediated tumor killing.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pembrolizumab is a PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor (blocking antibody). It blocks PD-1 on T cell surfaces, preventing tumor cells from evading immune detection through PD-L1 interaction. This reactivates T cell-mediated tumor killing. Pembrolizumab can be used regardless of specific PD-L1 expression status if other criteria are met. Common ADRs include fatigue, nausea, cough, decreased appetite, and skin rashes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: EGFR TKI inhibition is the mechanism of osimertinib, not pembrolizumab.
whyWrongByChoice.B: DNA cross-linking is the mechanism of cisplatin, not pembrolizumab.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ALK TKI inhibition is the mechanism of alectinib, not pembrolizumab.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Topoisomerase II inhibition is the mechanism of etoposide, not pembrolizumab.

questions.stem: Cisplatin is the most commonly used chemotherapy agent for lung and respiratory cancers. Which of the following best explains its preferential use for these cancers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is only active against squamous cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has affinity for lung cells and good lung tissue penetration

choices.label: C
choices.text: It has no side effects in lung cancer patients

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is the only available platinum agent

explanation.summary: Cisplatin is the most commonly used chemotherapy for lung cancers because it has affinity for lung cells and good penetration into lung tissue. Its side effects include ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity (the 'C's on chemoman').
explanation.whyCorrect: Cisplatin is the most commonly used chemotherapy for lung/respiratory cancers because it has an affinity for lung cells and good drug penetration into lung tissue. It is a platinum-based agent that causes direct DNA damage through intrastrand cross-linking. The most common side effects include ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cisplatin is active against many cell types, not only squamous cells. It is used across all lung cancer subtypes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cisplatin has significant side effects including ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Other platinum agents exist (carboplatin, oxaliplatin), but cisplatin is preferred for lung cancers due to its lung tissue affinity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cisplatin does not specifically target neuroendocrine cells. It causes DNA cross-linking in all rapidly dividing cells.

questions.stem: ALK-rearranged lung adenocarcinoma patients tend to have which of the following demographic characteristics?

explanation.summary: Patients with ALK-rearranged lung adenocarcinoma tend to be younger, light or nonsmokers. They are susceptible to specific ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitors like alectinib.
explanation.whyCorrect: ALK rearrangement in lung adenocarcinoma yields a new oncogene driving tumor growth. Patients with this rearrangement tend to be younger, light or nonsmokers, distinguishing them from the typical lung cancer patient population. They respond well to specific ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitors like alectinib.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Older heavy smokers are the typical demographic for all lung cancers, but ALK-rearranged patients specifically tend to be younger and lighter smokers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Elderly women with COPD are at risk for various lung cancers but are not the specific demographic for ALK-rearranged tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Asbestos workers are at risk for mesothelioma and lung cancer broadly, not specifically ALK-positive adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Immunocompromised patients are at risk for opportunistic infections and some cancers but are not specifically associated with ALK-rearranged adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most common tumor suppressor gene mutation found in squamous cell carcinoma of the lung?

explanation.summary: TP53 gene mutations are the most common genetic alteration in squamous cell carcinoma, found on chromosome 17. TP53 encodes a tumor suppressor protein that regulates the cell cycle and prevents tumor formation.
explanation.whyCorrect: TP53 mutations are the most common genetic alterations in squamous cell carcinoma of the lung. The TP53 gene encodes tumor protein p53, a critical tumor suppressor that regulates the cell cycle. Its inactivation allows unregulated cell-cycle progression leading to cancer. Other commonly mutated tumor suppressor genes in SCC include CDKN2 and RB1.

whyWrongByChoice.A: EGFR is a gain-of-function mutation in adenocarcinoma, not a tumor suppressor in SCC.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ALK rearrangement is found in adenocarcinoma, not SCC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: KRAS is an oncogene mutation in adenocarcinoma (25%), not the most common TSG mutation in SCC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MYC amplification is associated with SCLC, not SCC.

questions.stem: BRAF V600E mutations in adenocarcinoma are associated with which of the following clinical implications?

choices.label: B
choices.text: More aggressive disease and reduced survival

explanation.summary: BRAF V600E mutations in lung adenocarcinoma are associated with more aggressive disease and reduced survival. BRAF inhibitors can be used as targeted therapy for these patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: BRAF V600E mutations in adenocarcinoma are associated with a more aggressive disease course and reduced survival. However, these mutations are actionableâ€”BRAF inhibitors (dabrafenib) combined with MEK inhibitors (trametinib) can be used as targeted therapy. Knowing the BRAF status guides treatment decisions.

whyWrongByChoice.A: BRAF V600E mutations are associated with worse outcomes, not improved response to standard chemotherapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BRAF-mutant tumors do not spontaneously regress. They tend to be aggressive.
whyWrongByChoice.D: BRAF V600E mutations are associated with more aggressive disease, not lower metastatic risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: BRAF mutations are treated with BRAF inhibitors, not ALK inhibitors. These are different molecular targets.

questions.stem: When lung adenocarcinoma is diagnosed, molecular/genetic testing is needed. Which of the following best explains why this testing is critical?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To determine if the tumor is primary or metastatic

choices.label: B
choices.text: To identify mutations susceptible to targeted therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: To confirm the diagnosis on imaging

choices.label: D
choices.text: To replace the need for biopsy

explanation.summary: Molecular testing of adenocarcinoma identifies mutations (EGFR, ALK, BRAF, KRAS, ROS1, MET) that guide targeted therapy selection. This 'precision medicine' approach can offer better outcomes than conventional chemotherapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: When adenocarcinoma is diagnosed, molecular/genetic testing is needed to identify mutations susceptible to targeted therapy (e.g., EGFR â†’ osimertinib, ALK â†’ alectinib, BRAF â†’ BRAF inhibitors). This 'precision medicine' approach matches treatment to the specific molecular driver of the patient's cancer, often providing better outcomes than conventional chemotherapy alone.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Distinguishing primary from metastatic cancer uses immunohistochemistry (TTF-1, etc.), not the molecular testing for therapeutic targets.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Imaging identifies the tumor but molecular testing guides treatment, not imaging confirmation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Molecular testing supplements biopsyâ€”it does not replace the need for tissue diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Smoking status is determined by clinical history, not molecular testing.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man who currently smokes has a 22-pack-year history. He asks about lung cancer screening. Which strategy is recommended?

explanation.summary: USPSTF screens high-risk adults with annual LDCT.
explanation.whyCorrect: Age 50-80, 20+ pack-years, current or quit <15 years: annual LDCT.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CXR is not used for screening.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Screening is annual, not biennial.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sputum cytology is not a screening modality.
whyWrongByChoice.E: He meets all high-risk criteria.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with a 25-pack-year history asks about screening. Which factor disqualifies him from annual LDCT under current US guidelines?

explanation.summary: Screening begins at age 50.
explanation.whyCorrect: The minimum screening age is 50; he is 48.

whyWrongByChoice.B: 25 pack-years exceeds the 20 threshold.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Current smokers qualify.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sex is not a criterion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Screening targets asymptomatic patients.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old former smoker quit 12 years ago with a 30-pack-year history. Is he eligible for LDCT screening?

explanation.summary: He falls within all eligibility parameters.
explanation.whyCorrect: Age 50-80, 20+ pack-years, quit within 15 years.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 67 is within the 50-80 range.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 12 years is within the 15-year window.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 30 exceeds 20 pack-years.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Former smokers who quit <15 years qualify.

questions.stem: A chest CT in a 65-year-old smoker shows multiple bilateral lower-lobe nodules. Which is the most likely origin?

explanation.summary: Multiple lower-lobe lesions suggest metastasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Metastases typically present as multiple lesions in lower lobes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Primary tumors are usually singular, upper lobe.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Primary tumors are typically singular, upper lobe.
whyWrongByChoice.D: SCLC is usually a central primary mass.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hamartomas are typically solitary and benign.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with a solitary 2.5 cm upper-lobe nodule has a 35-pack-year history. What is the approximate likelihood this nodule is malignant?

explanation.summary: Size, age, and smoking strongly predict malignancy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nodules >2 cm in patients >60 who smoke are malignant >50% of the time.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This underestimates risk in this high-risk profile.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Applies to young nonsmokers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Too low for >2 cm nodule in older smoker.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Not certain without biopsy.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with lung cancer develops facial/arm swelling worse when supine, with headache. Which structure is compressed?

explanation.summary: Classic SVC syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: SVC compression reduces upper-body venous return causing facial/arm edema.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Phrenic compression causes hemidiaphragm paralysis, not edema.
whyWrongByChoice.B: RLN compression causes hoarseness.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Brachial plexus invasion causes arm pain (Pancoast).
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sympathetic compression causes Horner syndrome.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old smoker has apical lung tumor with right arm pain, ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis. Which nerve structure explains the eye findings?

explanation.summary: Pancoast tumor invades sympathetic chain producing Horner syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: Compression of sympathetic trunk yields miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: CN III palsy causes mydriasis and down-out gaze.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Trigeminal gives facial sensory loss.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vagus compression causes hoarseness.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phrenic causes diaphragm paralysis.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with SCLC has Na of 122 mEq/L with confusion. Which mechanism is most likely?

explanation.summary: SIADH is classic in SCLC.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC secretes ADH causing renal water retention and hyponatremia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PTHrP causes hypercalcemia, not hyponatremia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ectopic ACTH causes Cushingoid features.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Marrow invasion causes cytopenias.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lambert-Eaton causes weakness.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a central cavitating hilar mass has Ca of 13.8 mg/dL. What is the most likely cancer subtype?

explanation.summary: PTHrP-mediated hypercalcemia is most common in squamous cell.
explanation.whyCorrect: Squamous lung cancer classically secretes PTHrP.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenocarcinoma less often causes PTHrP hypercalcemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: SCLC causes SIADH, ectopic ACTH, Lambert-Eaton.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Large cell is less commonly linked to PTHrP.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carcinoids secrete serotonin.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old smoker has progressive proximal leg weakness that improves with repeated effort and dry mouth. Which tumor is most likely?

explanation.summary: Lambert-Eaton is most associated with SCLC.
explanation.whyCorrect: Autoantibodies in SCLC attack presynaptic Ca channels causing LEMS.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenocarcinoma rarely causes LEMS.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Squamous is linked to PTHrP.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mesothelioma is pleural, not paraneoplastic.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hamartoma is benign with no paraneoplastic syndrome.

questions.stem: An adenocarcinoma specimen is tested for actionable mutations. Which mutation most commonly guides targeted therapy in adenocarcinoma?

explanation.summary: EGFR gain-of-function is targetable in adenocarcinoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: EGFR TKIs treat EGFR-mutated adenocarcinoma.

whyWrongByChoice.B: MYC amplification is seen in SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: RB1 loss is not used as targeted marker here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: BCR-ABL is CML.
whyWrongByChoice.E: JAK2 is in myeloproliferative disease.

questions.stem: Which oncogenic change is most characteristic of small cell lung cancer?

explanation.summary: MYC amplification drives SCLC proliferation.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC is classically linked to MYC oncogene amplification.

whyWrongByChoice.A: EGFR is in adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ALK is in adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BRAF is in adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: KRAS is in adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: A shipyard worker with 30 years of asbestos exposure also smokes. How does this exposure profile alter lung cancer risk?

explanation.summary: Asbestos and smoking synergistically raise lung cancer risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Combined exposure substantially increases risk beyond either alone.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Effect is multiplicative, not simply additive.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Both are independent risk factors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Asbestos raises, not reduces, risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Both lung cancer and mesothelioma risks rise.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman never-smoker has a new 2 cm upper-lobe nodule. What is the best next step?

explanation.summary: Nonsmokers still develop lung cancer; nodules need workup.
explanation.whyCorrect: 10% of lung cancers occur in nonsmokers; evaluation is required.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lung cancer cannot be excluded based on smoking alone.
whyWrongByChoice.C: No evidence of infection provided.
whyWrongByChoice.D: She does not fit screening criteria; nodule already found.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PFTs do not diagnose cancer.

questions.stem: Biopsy in a 66-year-old smoker reveals adenocarcinoma. Per the brick, which modality best detects small tumors and lymph node invasion for staging?

explanation.summary: PET surpasses CT for detecting small lesions and nodal involvement.
explanation.whyCorrect: PET scanning is better than CT for small tumors and lymph node invasion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CXR has low sensitivity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CT is inferior to PET for small lesions.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ultrasound is not used for staging.
whyWrongByChoice.E: V/Q scans assess PE.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with lung cancer develops new unilateral leg swelling and pleuritic chest pain. Which mechanism best explains this?

explanation.summary: Cancer causes hypercoagulability via tissue factor and cysteine proteases.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tumor-secreted procoagulants predispose to DVT/PE.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SIADH causes hyponatremia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PTHrP causes hypercalcemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phrenic compression causes diaphragm paralysis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SVC obstruction causes upper body edema.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old man with NSCLC has new onset hoarseness without airway lesion on laryngoscopy. Which structure is most likely compressed?

explanation.summary: Hoarseness from vocal cord paralysis suggests RLN/vagus compression.
explanation.whyCorrect: Compression of vagus (supplying vocal cords) causes hoarseness.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Phrenic causes dyspnea, not hoarseness.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Brachial plexus causes arm pain.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Esophageal compression causes dysphagia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sympathetic compression causes Horner.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old with adenocarcinoma develops clubbing, periosteal long-bone pain, and symmetric arthritis. Which syndrome is most likely?

explanation.summary: HPOA triad: clubbing, periosteal pain, arthritis.
explanation.whyCorrect: HPOA is classic musculoskeletal paraneoplastic syndrome in adenocarcinoma.

whyWrongByChoice.A: LEMS causes muscle weakness, not clubbing.
whyWrongByChoice.C: SIADH causes hyponatremia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cushing causes truncal obesity, weakness.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DM causes skin rash, proximal weakness.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with SCLC has elevated cortisol, truncal obesity, and hypokalemia. Which mechanism is most likely?

explanation.summary: SCLC can secrete ectopic ACTH causing Cushingoid features.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ectopic ACTH from SCLC drives cortisol release from adrenals.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adrenal mets usually reduce, not increase, cortisol production.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PTHrP causes hypercalcemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: SIADH causes hyponatremia without hypercortisolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Not a recognized mechanism here.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with NSCLC complains of new bone pain. Which blood test abnormality is most suggestive of bone metastasis?

explanation.summary: ALP rises with bony or hepatic metastasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Elevated ALP may indicate bone or liver metastasis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Low sodium suggests SIADH.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Isolated ALT elevation suggests hepatocyte injury, less specific for bone.
whyWrongByChoice.D: High hemoglobin is not typical of metastasis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypokalemia suggests ectopic ACTH.

questions.stem: Following a confirmed biopsy of NSCLC, the patient reports new headaches and focal weakness. What is the best next step?

explanation.summary: CNS symptoms warrant brain MRI during staging.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with CNS symptoms receive brain or spinal MRI.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chest CT does not evaluate brain.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Abdominal US would not address CNS symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Marrow biopsy does not evaluate brain metastases.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bronchoscopy is diagnostic, not staging for CNS.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old smoker has a pulmonary mass, cervical lymphadenopathy on exam, and biopsy pending. Which study best confirms malignancy and guides targeted therapy?

explanation.summary: Tissue biopsy is essential for diagnosis and molecular profiling.
explanation.whyCorrect: Obtaining lung tissue is essential to diagnose and guide genetic-targeted therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cytology is less sensitive and lacks tissue for molecular testing.
whyWrongByChoice.B: PET stages but does not yield tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Markers do not establish diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CXR cannot confirm cancer.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with progressive left-sided nasal obstruction, intermittent epistaxis, and new anosmia. MRI shows a mass extending through the cribriform plate. Biopsy reveals small round blue cells in lobular nests within fibrovascular stroma, with Homer-Wright pseudorosettes. From which tissue does this tumor arise?

explanation.summary: Olfactory neuroblastoma arises from the olfactory epithelium.
explanation.whyCorrect: ONB (esthesioneuroblastoma) is a neuroendocrine tumor arising from olfactory epithelium in the nasal cavity, explaining the anosmia from cribriform plate extension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Salivary gland tissue gives rise to salivary neoplasms, not ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Respiratory epithelium lines much of the airway but is not the origin of ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Squamous epithelium origin is seen in laryngeal SCC, not ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nasopharyngeal mucosa gives rise to nasopharyngeal carcinoma.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with a sinonasal mass is found to have hyponatremia with serum sodium 122 mEq/L, urine osmolality 600 mOsm/kg, and normal volume status. Biopsy of the nasal mass shows small round blue cells with a lobular architecture in a fibrovascular stroma. What is the most likely underlying mechanism for his sodium abnormality?

explanation.summary: ONB can produce paraneoplastic SIADH.
explanation.whyCorrect: Olfactory neuroblastoma rarely produces paraneoplastic syndromes including SIADH (ectopic ADH) causing euvolemic hyponatremia with concentrated urine.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adrenal insufficiency is not typical of ONB paraneoplastic syndromes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PTHrP causes hypercalcemia, not hyponatremia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ectopic ACTH causes Cushing syndrome, not this euvolemic hyponatremia pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cerebral salt wasting shows volume depletion, not the euvolemic pattern here.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman presents with nasal obstruction, epistaxis, and right-sided proptosis with diplopia. Biopsy of a nasal mass shows neuroblasts and neurofibrils in lobular nests within a fibrovascular stroma. What is the most likely explanation for her ocular findings?

explanation.summary: ONB can extend into the orbit causing proptosis and diplopia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Orbital extension of olfactory neuroblastoma causes pain, proptosis, diplopia, and excessive tearing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Optic nerve glioma is unrelated to sinonasal small round blue cell tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Graves ophthalmopathy is autoimmune and unrelated to this biopsy finding.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cavernous sinus thrombosis presents with multiple cranial neuropathies and sepsis-like features.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carotid-cavernous fistula causes pulsatile exophthalmos and bruit, not this biopsy finding.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with biopsy-confirmed olfactory neuroblastoma has imaging showing local invasion into the ethmoid sinuses and orbit without distant metastases. What is the most appropriate treatment approach?

explanation.summary: ONB is treated with surgery combined with radiation and/or chemotherapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: The standard treatment for olfactory neuroblastoma is surgical resection combined with adjuvant radiation, chemotherapy, or both.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Radiation alone is inadequate; surgical excision is a cornerstone.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Immunotherapy has not shown substantial benefit in ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Targeted therapy has not shown substantial benefit in ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Observation is inappropriate for an aggressive invasive tumor.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A pathologist reviews a biopsy from a sinonasal mass and identifies small round blue cells in a lobular pattern with nests of neuroblasts and neurofibrils embedded in a vascular fibrous stroma. Which additional histologic feature would most strongly support the diagnosis of olfactory neuroblastoma?

explanation.summary: Homer-Wright pseudorosettes are characteristic of neuroblastomas including ONB.
explanation.whyCorrect: Homer-Wright pseudorosettes and Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes are characteristic pathologic features of neuroblastomas.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Keratin pearls are seen in squamous cell carcinoma (laryngeal cancer).
whyWrongByChoice.C: Psammoma bodies are seen in papillary thyroid cancer and meningiomas.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reed-Sternberg cells are seen in Hodgkin lymphoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Signet ring cells are characteristic of gastric adenocarcinoma.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 40-year-old man from Southern China presents with headaches, diplopia, right facial numbness, and a neck mass. Nasal endoscopy reveals a nasopharyngeal mass; biopsy shows nests of epithelioid cells lacking keratinization, with prominent nucleoli and infiltrating lymphocytes. Which virus is most strongly associated with this malignancy?

explanation.summary: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma is strongly associated with EBV.
explanation.whyCorrect: EBV is a major risk factor for nasopharyngeal carcinoma, particularly the non-keratinizing undifferentiated type seen in endemic regions.

whyWrongByChoice.A: HPV is associated with laryngeal and oropharyngeal cancer, not NPC.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CMV is not linked to NPC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HHV-8 causes Kaposi sarcoma, not NPC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HCV is linked to hepatocellular carcinoma, not NPC.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 50-year-old man of Southeast Asian origin reports a lifelong diet high in salted fish and preserved foods. He now presents with nasal obstruction, epistaxis, and a cervical lymph node mass. Which dietary component is thought to contribute to his cancer risk?

explanation.summary: Preserved foods (salted fish) contain nitrosamines linked to NPC.
explanation.whyCorrect: Eating preserved foods such as salted fish leads to high levels of nitrosamines, a known risk factor for nasopharyngeal carcinoma.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aflatoxins are linked to hepatocellular carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PAHs are linked to lung cancer from smoking/pollution.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Heterocyclic amines form during cooking meat and are linked more to colon cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acrylamide is formed in fried starchy foods and not specifically tied to NPC.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman has biopsy-confirmed non-keratinizing undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma that has spread to regional lymph nodes. Which is the most appropriate initial treatment?

explanation.summary: NPC is treated with radiotherapy and chemotherapy, not surgery.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma is uniquely treated with radiation and chemotherapy (cisplatin + gemcitabine); surgery is very specialized and challenging due to anatomic location.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Surgery is not the primary treatment for NPC because of anatomic complexity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Immunotherapy alone is not first-line therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Observation is inappropriate for invasive disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stem cell transplant is not used for NPC.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with nasopharyngeal carcinoma begins chemotherapy with cisplatin and gemcitabine. She develops myelosuppression, fatigue, hair loss, and elevated liver enzymes. Which best describes gemcitabine's mechanism of action?

explanation.summary: Gemcitabine is a pyrimidine analog antimetabolite.
explanation.whyCorrect: Gemcitabine is an antimetabolite chemotherapy, a pyrimidine analog similar to cytarabine that inhibits DNA synthesis during the S phase.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alkylating/cross-linking is the mechanism of cisplatin, not gemcitabine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Topoisomerase II inhibitors include etoposide, not gemcitabine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Microtubule stabilizers include paclitaxel.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Folate antagonists include methotrexate.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a 50-pack-year smoking history presents with 6 months of progressive hoarseness, a tickling cough, and an enlarged left neck lymph node. Laryngoscopy identifies a mass on the vocal cord. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: The presentation is classic for laryngeal SCC in a smoker.
explanation.whyCorrect: Persistent hoarseness, cough, and cervical lymphadenopathy in a long-term smoker strongly suggest laryngeal cancer, most commonly squamous cell carcinoma.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ONB presents with nasal obstruction and anosmia, not hoarseness.
whyWrongByChoice.C: NPC typically presents with cranial nerve findings and nasal obstruction, not vocal cord mass.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adenoid cystic carcinoma is uncommon and not characterized by this scenario.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sinonasal lymphoma would not present with a vocal cord mass.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 70-year-old man is diagnosed with laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma. Which anatomic subsite accounts for the majority of laryngeal cancers?

explanation.summary: Supraglottic tumors account for two-thirds of laryngeal cancers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Laryngeal cancers are classified as supraglottic (two-thirds of cases), glottic (one-third), or subglottic (1-2%).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Glottic tumors account for about one-third, not the majority.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Subglottic tumors represent only 1-2% of cases.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pyriform sinus is part of the hypopharynx, not a standard laryngeal subsite category.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Postcricoid is a hypopharyngeal subsite, not a laryngeal subsite category.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 35-year-old non-smoker presents with hoarseness. Laryngoscopy reveals a glottic mass; biopsy shows squamous cell carcinoma. Which viral oncoprotein most likely drives this cancer by inactivating p53?

explanation.summary: HPV E6 inactivates p53.
explanation.whyCorrect: HPV E6 inactivates the tumor suppressor p53, while E7 inactivates pRb; together they drive uncontrolled cell cycle progression leading to laryngeal/oropharyngeal cancer.

whyWrongByChoice.A: E7 inactivates pRb, not p53.
whyWrongByChoice.B: LMP1 is associated with EBV-driven NPC, not p53 inactivation in laryngeal SCC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: vGPCR is associated with Kaposi sarcoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HTLV-1 Tax drives adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with laryngeal SCC has a tumor localized to the larynx without nodal spread. Which treatment strategy best preserves vocal function?

explanation.summary: Localized laryngeal cancer is treated with radiation plus limited (organ-sparing) surgery.
explanation.whyCorrect: For tumors localized to the larynx, combined radiation and limited surgery (eg, partial laryngectomy) are used to preserve vocal function.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Total laryngectomy is reserved for advanced disease and sacrifices voice.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Chemotherapy alone is not the standard for localized disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Immunotherapy alone is not first-line for localized laryngeal cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Observation risks progression of a curable localized cancer.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A pathologist examines a laryngectomy specimen. Which microscopic feature is most characteristic of laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Small round blue cells in lobules

explanation.summary: Laryngeal SCC shows keratin pearls, polygonal cells, and pleomorphic nuclei.
explanation.whyCorrect: Laryngeal SCC pathology typically shows polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm, keratin pearls, and pleomorphic nuclei.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Small round blue cells in lobules describe olfactory neuroblastoma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Syncytial clusters with lymphocytes describe non-keratinizing NPC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glandular tubules with mucin are features of adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Spindle cells with herringbone pattern describe fibrosarcoma.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 62-year-old smoker with laryngeal SCC has biopsy-proven spread to cervical lymph nodes. Which is the most likely chemotherapy agent to be added to his regimen?

explanation.summary: Cisplatin is the most common chemotherapy for laryngeal cancer.
explanation.whyCorrect: When laryngeal cancer spreads to adjacent neck structures, chemotherapy is added; the most common agent used is cisplatin.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Gemcitabine is paired with cisplatin for NPC, not laryngeal cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Etoposide is used with cisplatin for ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Methotrexate is not the standard for laryngeal cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vincristine is not standard for laryngeal cancer.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with unilateral serous otitis media and ear pain. Nasal endoscopy reveals a nasopharyngeal mass. Which anatomic structure is most likely obstructed by the tumor, causing her ear symptoms?

explanation.summary: Nasopharyngeal tumors obstruct the Eustachian tube causing otitis media.
explanation.whyCorrect: Obstruction of the Eustachian tube by a tumor causes ear pain and otitis media, a classic presentation of nasopharyngeal/sinonasal tumors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: External auditory canal is not obstructed by nasopharyngeal masses.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The round window is intracranial and not obstructed by nasopharyngeal masses.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The oval window is part of middle ear ossicle transmission, not obstructed externally.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The cochlear aqueduct connects inner ear to subarachnoid space, not nasopharynx.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with biopsy-proven olfactory neuroblastoma will begin chemotherapy. Which combination regimen is most commonly used for this tumor?

explanation.summary: ONB is typically treated with cisplatin + etoposide.
explanation.whyCorrect: The most common chemotherapy regimen for olfactory neuroblastoma is cisplatin plus etoposide.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cisplatin + gemcitabine is the regimen for nasopharyngeal carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cyclophosphamide + doxorubicin is used for lymphomas/breast cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Carboplatin + paclitaxel is common in NSCLC and ovarian cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Methotrexate + 5-FU is not the standard ONB regimen.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with hoarseness, dysphagia, and odynophagia for several months along with recent weight loss. Which finding most strongly suggests metastatic disease rather than local complications?

explanation.summary: Rapid weight loss suggests metastatic laryngeal cancer.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rapid weight loss is described as a feature of metastatic laryngeal cancer; the other symptoms reflect local disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Persistent cough occurs with localized laryngeal cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Voice change typically reflects local vocal cord involvement.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Odynophagia reflects local invasion, not metastases.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dysphagia reflects local extension, not metastases.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 50-year-old shipyard worker with a 30-pack-year smoking history presents with hoarseness and is diagnosed with laryngeal SCC. Besides tobacco, which occupational exposure most likely contributed to his cancer?

explanation.summary: Occupational asbestos exposure is a risk factor for laryngeal cancer.
explanation.whyCorrect: Occupational exposure to asbestos, radiation, paint, and gas fumes are cited risk factors for laryngeal cancer.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Silica is linked primarily to silicosis and lung cancer, not specifically laryngeal cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Coal dust is associated with pneumoconiosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beryllium causes berylliosis, not specifically laryngeal cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cotton dust causes byssinosis, not laryngeal cancer.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 30-year-old man from North Africa presents with a left neck mass, diplopia, and facial numbness. Endoscopic biopsy shows syncytial clusters of malignant epithelial cells infiltrated by benign lymphocytes, positive for EBV. Which histologic subtype of nasopharyngeal carcinoma is this?

explanation.summary: The non-keratinizing undifferentiated type is most common and EBV-associated.
explanation.whyCorrect: Non-keratinizing undifferentiated NPC (60-65% of cases) shows syncytial clusters of tumor cells with lymphocytic infiltrate and strong EBV association.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Keratinizing type accounts for 20-25% and shows more keratinization.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Basaloid squamous type is rare (1-2%).
whyWrongByChoice.D: Non-keratinizing differentiated type is less common (10-15%).
whyWrongByChoice.E: Adenosquamous is not among the four common NPC subtypes.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with progressive unilateral nasal obstruction and epistaxis. MRI shows a sinonasal mass extending through the cribriform plate into the anterior cranial fossa. Biopsy shows lobular small round blue cells in fibrovascular stroma. Which paraneoplastic syndrome is LEAST likely associated with his tumor?

explanation.summary: ONB is associated with SIADH, hypercalcemia, and ectopic ACTH but not cerebellar degeneration.
explanation.whyCorrect: Olfactory neuroblastoma rarely causes paraneoplastic syndromes including SIADH, hypercalcemia, and ectopic ACTH; paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration is not described.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SIADH is a recognized paraneoplastic syndrome of ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ectopic ACTH is a described paraneoplastic syndrome in ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypercalcemia is a described paraneoplastic syndrome in ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hyponatremia (from SIADH) is an expected ONB paraneoplastic finding.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with hoarseness is found to have a supraglottic mass on laryngoscopy, and biopsy confirms SCC. Which is the most common site of metastasis for laryngeal cancer?

explanation.summary: Laryngeal cancer commonly metastasizes to local lymph nodes.
explanation.whyCorrect: All laryngeal cancer subsites commonly metastasize to local lymph nodes; distant metastases occur but are less common initially.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lung metastases occur but are less common than local lymph node spread.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Liver is not the most common site of laryngeal cancer metastasis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bone metastases occur in late/advanced disease, not most commonly.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Brain metastases are uncommon in laryngeal cancer.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history is brought in by his daughter for worsening confusion over the past month. He has lost 15 lb. He is oriented only to place. Labs reveal serum sodium of 117 mEq/L. Chest x-ray shows a perihilar mass. Head CT is normal. Which mechanism best explains his hyponatremia?

explanation.summary: SIADH from SCLC causes euvolemic hyponatremia.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC cells ectopically secrete ADH (SIADH), causing water retention and dilutional hyponatremia; seen in ~10% of SCLC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ACTH causes Cushing syndrome, not hyponatremia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Head CT is normal; no intracranial pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adrenal metastases cause hyperkalemia and volume depletion, not SIADH pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PTHrP causes hypercalcemia (squamous cell), not hyponatremia.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old smoker is diagnosed with SCLC. Biopsy shows small cells with scant cytoplasm, finely granular chromatin with interspersed lighter areas, and inconspicuous nucleoli. This characteristic chromatin pattern is most specifically associated with which cell lineage?

explanation.summary: Salt-and-pepper chromatin is a hallmark of neuroendocrine tumors.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC arises from bronchial neuroendocrine cells; salt-and-pepper chromatin is characteristic of neuroendocrine neoplasms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Squamous cancers show keratin pearls and intercellular bridges.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Adenocarcinoma shows glandular/mucinous features.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mesothelioma has distinct papillary architecture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lymphoid cells lack this chromatin pattern.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with SCLC develops truncal obesity, purple striae, and proximal muscle weakness. Labs show hypokalemia and hyperglycemia. Which ectopic hormone best explains these findings?

explanation.summary: Ectopic ACTH causes paraneoplastic Cushing syndrome in SCLC.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC secretes ACTH in 1-5% of patients, causing Cushing syndrome with weight gain, muscle weakness, thin/fragile skin.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ADH causes SIADH/hyponatremia, not Cushing features.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PTHrP is associated with squamous cell lung cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Calcitonin is associated with medullary thyroid cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Serotonin relates to carcinoid syndrome.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old smoker with SCLC complains of proximal muscle weakness that improves with repeated exertion. Which autoantibody is most likely responsible?

explanation.summary: LEMS in SCLC is mediated by antibodies against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome involves antibodies to presynaptic calcium channels, causing decreased ACh release; strength improves with repeated use.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anti-AChR causes myasthenia gravis (worsens with use).
whyWrongByChoice.B: Anti-MuSK is another myasthenia gravis variant.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anti-Hu causes paraneoplastic encephalomyelitis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anti-Yo causes cerebellar degeneration.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man is diagnosed with extensive-stage SCLC. Which initial treatment approach is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: Extensive-stage SCLC is treated primarily with systemic chemotherapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cisplatin + etoposide is the standard first-line regimen for SCLC, used alone in extensive disease to slow growth and improve survival.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Surgery is almost never appropriate; most patients have metastases.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Radiation alone is insufficient for widespread disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: EGFR inhibitors are used in NSCLC, not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SCLC is too aggressive to observe.

questions.stem: A pathologist examining an SCLC biopsy notes basophilic DNA deposits encrusting small blood vessel walls adjacent to areas of tumor necrosis. What is this finding called?

explanation.summary: The Azzopardi effect reflects DNA encrustation of vessel walls in high-grade tumors.
explanation.whyCorrect: In SCLC, necrotic tumor cells release DNA that adheres to blood vessel walls, producing basophilic encrustation known as the Azzopardi effect.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Crush artifact distorts cells mechanically; not vessel encrustation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Psammoma bodies are laminated calcifications.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Schiller-Duval bodies occur in yolk sac tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Call-Exner bodies occur in granulosa cell tumors.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old smoker is found to have a centrally located lung mass. To definitively diagnose suspected SCLC while minimizing risk to mediastinal structures, which procedure is preferred?

explanation.summary: Central SCLC masses are best biopsied bronchoscopically.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC is typically central; bronchoscopy accesses it safely. Transthoracic needle biopsy risks damaging mediastinal structures.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Transthoracic needle biopsy risks injury to central mediastinal vessels.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Open surgery is not first-line for diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sputum cytology is insensitive for definitive diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Most patients lack diagnostic pleural effusion.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with SCLC is started on chemotherapy. His sodium is 118 mEq/L and he is acutely confused with a seizure. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

explanation.summary: Severe symptomatic hyponatremia requires hypertonic saline.
explanation.whyCorrect: Symptomatic severe hyponatremia (confusion, seizures) requires hypertonic saline to rapidly but carefully raise serum sodium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fluid restriction is too slow for seizures.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Isotonic saline may worsen SIADH-related hyponatremia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Loop diuretic alone is inadequate monotherapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chemo effect takes weeks; patient needs immediate intervention.

questions.stem: An SCLC tumor shows gene amplification driving uncontrolled proliferation. Which oncogene is most commonly amplified in SCLC?

explanation.summary: MYC is commonly amplified in SCLC.
explanation.whyCorrect: MYC amplification drives tumor cell proliferation in SCLC.

whyWrongByChoice.B: EGFR mutations characterize adenocarcinoma of the lung.
whyWrongByChoice.C: KRAS is common in NSCLC adenocarcinoma, not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HER2 is relevant to breast/gastric cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ALK rearrangements occur in NSCLC.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old smoker with SCLC has immunohistochemistry performed on biopsy tissue. Which pair of markers most strongly supports the neuroendocrine origin?

explanation.summary: Chromogranin and synaptophysin are classic neuroendocrine markers.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC tumor cells express chromogranin and synaptophysin, confirming neuroendocrine differentiation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CK7/TTF-1 are nonspecific lung epithelial markers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: p40 and CK5/6 indicate squamous differentiation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Calretinin/WT-1 mark mesothelioma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CD20/CD79a mark B-cell lymphoma.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man is newly diagnosed with SCLC confined to one hemithorax and regional lymph nodes without distant metastasis. What is the expected median survival with treatment?

explanation.summary: Limited-stage SCLC has 15-20 month median survival.
explanation.whyCorrect: Limited-stage disease (one lung plus nearby nodes) has median survival of 15-20 months with chemoradiation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Too short; applies to untreated late disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Too short for limited stage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 8-13 months is expected for extensive-stage disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SCLC rarely has such long survival; mortality is high.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with SCLC is started on a chemotherapy agent that inhibits religation of double-strand DNA breaks during S/early G2 phase. Which drug is this?

explanation.summary: Etoposide is a topoisomerase II inhibitor.
explanation.whyCorrect: Etoposide inhibits topoisomerase II religation of double-strand DNA breaks during S/early G2 phases, core to SCLC therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cisplatin cross-links DNA; it does not inhibit topoisomerase II.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Paclitaxel stabilizes microtubules.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Doxorubicin intercalates DNA and generates free radicals.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Methotrexate inhibits dihydrofolate reductase.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old patient with SCLC on cisplatin-etoposide develops fatigue, pallor, easy bruising, and fevers. CBC shows pancytopenia. Which drug effect best explains this?

explanation.summary: Etoposide causes myelosuppression.
explanation.whyCorrect: Etoposide commonly causes myelosuppression, producing pancytopenia with fatigue, bruising, and infection risk.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nephrotoxicity is a cisplatin effect but doesn't cause pancytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ototoxicity is a cisplatin effect unrelated to CBC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Neuropathy is a cisplatin effect, not marrow related.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cardiotoxicity is characteristic of doxorubicin.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with SCLC has lesions on imaging raising concern for metastasis. Which combination represents the most common metastatic sites for SCLC?

explanation.summary: SCLC preferentially metastasizes to liver, adrenals, bone, marrow, and brain.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC has propensity for liver, adrenals, bone, bone marrow, and brain; ~70% present with overt metastases.

whyWrongByChoice.B: These are uncommon SCLC metastatic sites.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Skin is possible but thyroid/larynx aren't typical.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Not typical distribution.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Not characteristic sites.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old woman presents with a central bronchial neuroendocrine tumor causing wheezing and recurrent pneumonia. Biopsy shows well-differentiated cells with rosettes and trabecular patterns. This tumor is best classified as:

explanation.summary: Bronchial carcinoids are low-grade, well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bronchial carcinoids are well-differentiated, central neuroendocrine tumors causing obstructive symptoms, with rosette/trabecular patterns.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SCLC is poorly differentiated/high-grade, not trabecular/rosetted.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Squamous cell shows keratinization and pearls.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adenocarcinoma shows glandular patterns.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mesothelioma arises from pleura.

questions.stem: A molecular pathologist reviews the genetic profile of an SCLC tumor. Which pair of tumor suppressor genes is most commonly mutated?

explanation.summary: TP53 and RB are hallmark mutations in SCLC.
explanation.whyCorrect: Smoking-induced somatic mutations in TP53 and RB are commonly found in SCLC, enabling uncontrolled division and reduced apoptosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: APC/BRCA1 are colorectal/breast-related.
whyWrongByChoice.C: VHL/NF1 relate to renal/neurofibromatosis syndromes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: WT1/PTEN not characteristic of SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MLH1/MSH2 cause Lynch syndrome.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with SCLC-related SIADH is hyponatremic. After two weeks of chemotherapy, sodium normalizes. What is the underlying reason for resolution?

explanation.summary: Tumor cytoreduction reduces ectopic ADH secretion.
explanation.whyCorrect: As chemotherapy reduces tumor burden, ectopic ADH production falls, resolving SIADH-related hyponatremia over several weeks.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chemo does not directly stimulate V2 receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chemo does not cause DI.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Steroids aren't the mechanism of SIADH resolution here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Renal perfusion isn't the primary issue.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old former smoker has a solitary peripheral lung nodule without evidence of nodal or distant spread; biopsy reveals SCLC. Which treatment approach is most appropriate in this uncommon scenario?

explanation.summary: Isolated SCLC without metastasis may be resected with adjuvant chemo.
explanation.whyCorrect: A single lung mass without metastatic spread can rarely undergo surgical resection followed by postoperative chemotherapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SCLC is too aggressive to observe.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Monotherapy immunotherapy isn't standard first line for localized SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Palliative radiation is inadequate for potentially curable disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hormonal therapy has no role in SCLC.

questions.stem: SCLC represents approximately what percentage of all primary lung cancers?

explanation.summary: SCLC accounts for about 15% of primary lung cancers.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC represents roughly 15% of all primary lung cancers but is the most aggressive subtype.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Too low; 1% represents SCLC in nonsmokers.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Too low.
